question: What does the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) show, and what are the corresponding temperature values from Table 5.1-2?

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software modeling and hand calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring low pressure losses to support natural circulation, and confirms that steam generator tube plugging does not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document as needed for regulatory purposes, with all copies required to include the copyright notice.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is responsible for removing heat from the reactor core and transferring it to the steam generators using natural circulation, driven by density and elevation differences. The RCS includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated valves, and interfaces with several other systems to maintain safety and operational integrity, including the removal of noncondensible gases during both normal and emergency conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are systematically evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, heater sheaths, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures for ensuring the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel's resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation over its 60-year design life, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within regulatory safety criteria.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

edges.description: Section A discusses the evaluation of steady state parameters (such as temperature values) for the RCS, which are depicted schematically in the flow diagram referenced in Section B's summary description of the RCS and its components.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system parameters (such as temperature) for optimal performance, which are depicted in the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and quantified in Table 5.1-2, while Section B provides a general description of the RCS components shown in the diagram.

Section A provides a technical summary of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) design, which is the subject matter introduced in Section B's preface to the chapter, thereby specifying the content that Section B generally describes.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies particular system features (degasification line, RVVs) that implement and detail the functions described in Section A.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B explains how system parameters, including those relevant to the DHRS, are evaluated and established for optimal performance, connecting the DHRS operation to the steady-state values (such as temperatures) shown in the schematic and table.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's function to cool the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) under specific conditions, while Section B provides a general summary of the RCS design and components, with Section A specifying details for the general system described in Section B.

Section A discusses the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System within the Reactor Coolant System, which is the subject of the broader document introduced in Section B, thereby providing specific technical content for the general context set by Section B.

Section B discusses the establishment of steady state parameter values (such as temperature) for system evaluation, which are the same values depicted in the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and listed in Table 5.1-2 referenced in the analysis question.
Section A provides detailed descriptions of specific RCS components (such as the pressurizer and its materials), which are part of the general RCS system overview described in Section B.

Section A references specific sections and component descriptions within the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, which is introduced and contextually framed by the copyright and scope information in Section B's preface.

Section A describes operational steps and system component manipulations (such as isolating SGs and adjusting PZR water level) that are depicted schematically in the RCS Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3), while Section B discusses the evaluation of system performance and the establishment of steady-state parameter values (such as temperatures) that correspond to the diagram and are tabulated in Table 5.1-2.

Section A describes specific operational steps and component manipulations within the RCS, while Section B provides the general design basis and components of the RCS, with Section A detailing actions for components introduced in Section B.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, while Section B describes the establishment of steady state parameter values (such as temperature) for system optimization, thus Section B provides the design values that Section A's procedures must ensure are not exceeded.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, which relies on the design basis and system description provided in Section B for understanding the RCS configuration and operational parameters.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by not exceeding pressure-temperature limits, which are detailed in technical specifications found in Chapter Five as introduced in Section B's preface.

Section B discusses the establishment of steady state parameter values (such as temperature) for system evaluation, which are the specific values depicted in the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram and detailed in Table 5.1-2, while Section A addresses overpressure protection requirements that rely on these established system parameters.

Section A references regulatory compliance and code requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B provides a general design basis and summary description of the reactor coolant system, with Section A's requirements applying to the system described in Section B.

Section A discusses regulatory compliance for overpressure protection in the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B is the preface to the chapter containing the RCS schematic and temperature data, thus Section B provides the structural context and scope for the technical details referenced in Section A.

Section A describes system components and operational steps that affect reactor coolant system (RCS) conditions, while Section B discusses the need for operating procedures to ensure that pressure-temperature limits—such as those indicated by temperature values in Table 5.1-2—are not exceeded, thus linking the operational states shown in the schematic (Figure 5.1-3) to regulatory compliance.

Section A describes operational steps and system component manipulations relevant to the RCS, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that these system components and operations must comply with, connecting the design/operation in A to the regulatory requirements in B.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, while Section B outlines compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection requirements, connecting them through the shared objective of maintaining reactor coolant boundary integrity under regulatory limits.
